meta.note: Low Countries + Bulgaria + Lithuania remainder. Most candidates from this sweep were already in the DB (Nordic obscenity cases, most Bulgarian communist-era titles, both Koerbagh works) — only these six were genuinely new on dedup. Memoirs of a Woman of Pleasure = Fanny Hill (id 577, already has a Denmark ban). Belgian entries are title-specific morals prosecutions that ended in acquittal (action_type challenged).

entries.title: Fashizmut
entries.description_ban: Scholarly study of fascist regimes ('Фашизмът / Fascism'); about three weeks after release the regime pulled all copies from bookstores and libraries because its critique of totalitarianism read as applying to communism. It circulated underground and became a dissident touchstone.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific communist-era ban; documented via Wikipedia citing the Encyclopedia of Political Communication.

entries.title: De zoon van Dik Trom
entries.description_ban: Placed on the German-occupation banned-books list in October 1941 over an anti-German passage in which children play-acting Dutch-vs-German armies shout 'Weg met de Duitsers!'.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific occupation-era ban; documented by the Leiden4045 occupation-history project.

entries.title: Algemene Staatsleer
entries.description_ban: Constitutional-law textbook by a prominent Leiden jurist, banned by the German occupation's Sicherheitspolizei censorship; appears by name on the wartime prohibited-books list.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific occupation-era ban; documented by Leiden4045 (medium confidence).

entries.title: Escal-Vigor
entries.description_ban: One of the first European novels overtly defending homosexuality; prosecuted at Bruges in 1900 as pornography/an affront to morals. Eekhoud was acquitted after a writers' petition (a prosecution rather than a completed ban).
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific morals prosecution; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: L'Homme en amour
entries.description_ban: Naturalist novel charged by the Bruges prosecutor as an offence against public morals; Lemonnier was acquitted at trial (a prosecution/seizure attempt rather than a completed ban).
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific morals prosecution; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: Amber Heart
entries.description_ban: Children's fairy-tale collection (Gintarinė širdis); two stories depict same-sex relationships. Distribution was suspended in 2013, then re-released in 2014 with a label marking it harmful to under-14s. The ECtHR Grand Chamber (Macatė v. Lithuania, 2023) found the restriction violated Article 10.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific restriction confirmed by an ECtHR Grand Chamber judgment.
